questions.question: What is the first step in a useful consistency discussion?
options: Name the database vendor that supports the most replication modes.
Identify which records or operations truly require strict correctness and which can tolerate staleness.
Assume global strong consistency unless the interviewer says otherwise.
Defer all consistency trade-offs until after observability is designed.
questions.explanation: Consistency only becomes meaningful when attached to concrete operations and business guarantees.

questions.question: Why is eventual consistency not automatically wrong?
options: Because some read paths can trade freshness for latency, scale, or availability if the anomaly budget is acceptable.
Because eventual consistency guarantees zero stale reads after failover.
Because it removes the need for a source of truth.
Because Google interviews prefer any weaker guarantee by default.
questions.explanation: Weaker consistency can be appropriate when the product can tolerate specific anomalies.

questions.question: What is the strongest way to describe a bounded-staleness read path?
options: Reads may be slightly old, but they are usually fine in practice.
Replica reads are acceptable only if data is no more than the defined freshness budget behind the write authority.
Replica reads are always equivalent to the primary if caches are warm enough.
Read freshness is not worth measuring because it varies by region.
questions.explanation: Bounded staleness should be defined as an explicit contract, not a vague hope.

questions.question: What trade-off usually appears when strong cross-region write coordination is required?
options: Write latency and fault-tolerance flexibility often get worse because more synchronous coordination is needed.
Read path observability becomes unnecessary.
Sharding is no longer relevant anywhere in the system.
Caches become impossible to use.
questions.explanation: Synchronous coordination across regions typically raises latency and operational complexity.

questions.question: Which failure signal best detects surprising stale reads?
options: Replica freshness lag or version-skew metrics tied to the read path.
Only total CPU usage across the fleet.
The number of data stores shown in the diagram.
The average code review turnaround time.

questions.question: Why should a senior answer name at least one tolerated anomaly?
options: Because it proves the consistency model is tied to real user-visible behavior rather than label-only terminology.
Because interviewers require an anomaly even when the system is fully serialized.
Because tolerated anomalies eliminate the need for repair jobs.
Because anomalies matter only for financial systems.
questions.explanation: Naming an acceptable anomaly clarifies what the design optimizes for and what it intentionally does not.

questions.question: What is the main risk of a failover that promotes lagging state?
options: The new leader may expose stale or missing updates unless promotion gates and repair logic are in place.
Clients will stop using load balancers entirely.
The system will automatically gain stronger consistency.
It only affects logging pipelines, not product behavior.
questions.explanation: Failover safety depends on how far the promoted state is behind and how the system contains that gap.

questions.question: If the interviewer says globally visible writes must now appear within two seconds, what is the strongest response?
options: Identify which replication, cache, or routing decisions must tighten and what new latency or cost trade-off appears.
Keep the same eventual consistency story and hope the interviewer accepts it.
Remove observability because stronger consistency will simplify operations.
Change only the API names.
questions.explanation: A stronger freshness target should cause concrete architectural and operational changes.
